Advisor: Great. On income—roughly what’s the household gross income per year, all sources combined?
Client 2: I always think of it as, like, in the one-seventies? But David has the exact number.
Client 1: The household gross income annually is 173150.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And what makes that up—Social Security plus rental income, right?
Client 1: Yes. Social Security is 57800 a year, paid monthly, and the rental income is 115300 a year, also coming in monthly.

Client 2: Right, both retired.
Advisor: Okay. Switching to spending—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in?
Client 2: It’s pretty steady—total monthly expenses are 5400.
Advisor: Thanks. When you say all-in, that includes the usual bills, groceries, insurance, that kind of thing?
Client 2: Yes—our normal living costs. Not counting any big one-off trips.

Advisor: Great. On income—roughly what’s the household gross income per year, all sources combined?
Client 2: I always think of it as, like, in the one-seventies? But David has the exact number.
Client 1: The household gross income annually is 173150.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And what makes that up—Social Security plus rental income, right?
Client 1: Yes. Social Security is 57800 a year, paid monthly, and the rental income is 115300 a year, also coming in monthly.

Client 2: Yes, no loans.
Advisor: Great. Let’s talk assets next—investments and cash first, then the house. About how much do you have in investable assets total?
Client 1: Investable assets total are 1067100.
Advisor: Okay—hang on, I’m just noting that down. Within that, David, do you have a taxable investment account on an advisor platform?
Client 1: Yes. I have a taxable account on an advisor platform valued at 428450.

Client 2: Right. Private markets at a brokerage, 128900.
Advisor: Great. And then the home—your primary residence—what’s the value you’re using for that?
Client 2: The primary residence value is 342650, and it’s with the bank.
Advisor: Thanks. And no mortgage against it, correct?
Client 1: Correct—no mortgage.

Advisor: Great. On income—roughly what’s the household gross income per year, all sources combined?
Client 2: I always think of it as, like, in the one-seventies? But David has the exact number.
Client 1: The household gross income annually is 173150.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And what makes that up—Social Security plus rental income, right?
Client 1: Yes. Social Security is 57800 a year, paid monthly, and the rental income is 115300 a year, also coming in monthly.

Advisor: Great. On income—roughly what’s the household gross income per year, all sources combined?
Client 2: I always think of it as, like, in the one-seventies? But David has the exact number.
Client 1: The household gross income annually is 173150.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And what makes that up—Social Security plus rental income, right?
Client 1: Yes. Social Security is 57800 a year, paid monthly, and the rental income is 115300 a year, also coming in monthly.

Advisor: Great. On income—roughly what’s the household gross income per year, all sources combined?
Client 2: I always think of it as, like, in the one-seventies? But David has the exact number.
Client 1: The household gross income annually is 173150.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And what makes that up—Social Security plus rental income, right?
Client 1: Yes. Social Security is 57800 a year, paid monthly, and the rental income is 115300 a year, also coming in monthly.

Advisor: Perfect. Next—taxes. When you think about your marginal bracket, where do you usually land these days?
Client 2: I think it’s around thirty percent? I always forget which one we’re in.
Client 1: It’s 32%. We’re in the 32% bracket band.
Advisor: Got it—thank you, David. That helps me frame withdrawals and the rental income.
Advisor: And last admin item: for the relationship here—are you looking to work with an RIA, a registered investment advisor, versus, say, a brokerage-only setup?

Advisor: Hi David, hi Elizabeth—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would you most like this plan to do for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m David. We want something pretty straightforward—protect our lifestyle, keep taxes sensible, and not take unnecessary risk.
Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth—yeah, just… peace of mind. I don’t want it to feel complicated.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start with the cash coming in, then we’ll go through what you own, and any debts, and then insurance. Sound okay?
Client 1: Works for me.

Client 1: It’s 32%. We’re in the 32% bracket band.
Advisor: Got it—thank you, David. That helps me frame withdrawals and the rental income.
Advisor: And last admin item: for the relationship here—are you looking to work with an RIA, a registered investment advisor, versus, say, a brokerage-only setup?
Client 2: Yes, we want an RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Okay, that’s clear. One sec—just noting that down.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Elizabeth—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would you most like this plan to do for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m David. We want something pretty straightforward—protect our lifestyle, keep taxes sensible, and not take unnecessary risk.
Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth—yeah, just… peace of mind. I don’t want it to feel complicated.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start with the cash coming in, then we’ll go through what you own, and any debts, and then insurance. Sound okay?
Client 1: Works for me.

Advisor: Great—thanks. Now that we’ve got the big buckets down, I want to confirm a couple household basics that affect planning, like benefits, taxes, and how we frame risk.
Advisor: First—just to make sure I’m using the right language—David and Elizabeth, what’s your marital status?
Client 1: We’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And dependents—anyone financially dependent on you two right now? Kids you still support, grandkids, anyone like that?
Client 2: No—none. Number of dependents is 0.

Advisor: Great. On income—roughly what’s the household gross income per year, all sources combined?
Client 2: I always think of it as, like, in the one-seventies? But David has the exact number.
Client 1: The household gross income annually is 173150.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And what makes that up—Social Security plus rental income, right?
Client 1: Yes. Social Security is 57800 a year, paid monthly, and the rental income is 115300 a year, also coming in monthly.

Client 2: No—none. Number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. Next one’s a little clunky: what state do you consider your primary residence for planning purposes?
Client 2: It’s… not one of the usual ones you list. Put state of residence as Other.
Advisor: Okay—Other. That works. And just so I don’t assume: you’re not splitting time in a way that changes that, right?
Client 1: No, it’s straightforward—state of residence is Other.

Client 2: I get nervous, honestly. I like things steady, but I’m not trying to be all cash either.
Advisor: That makes sense. David—how would you label it if you had to pick a single word?
Client 1: Moderate. Risk tolerance is moderate.
Advisor: Okay, moderate—thank you. And Elizabeth, does “moderate” feel right to you too, or would you say a notch more cautious?
Client 2: Moderate is fine—maybe moderate-to-cautious emotionally, but yes, moderate.

Client 2: I usually think of it as around forty thousand a year.
Advisor: Yep—around forty. If we’re pinning it down for the plan, what number should I use?
Client 2: Use 40800 as my gross annual income.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And that’s gross before taxes, just to be clear.
Client 2: Yes—gross, before taxes.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Elizabeth—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would you most like this plan to do for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m David. We want something pretty straightforward—protect our lifestyle, keep taxes sensible, and not take unnecessary risk.
Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth—yeah, just… peace of mind. I don’t want it to feel complicated.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start with the cash coming in, then we’ll go through what you own, and any debts, and then insurance. Sound okay?
Client 1: Works for me.

Advisor: Perfect. And just so I’m attributing it correctly—David, the rental income is yours, and Social Security is joint between you two?
Client 1: Correct—rental is mine, Social Security is joint.
Advisor: And you’re both retired at this point, no wages or anything like that?
Client 2: Right, both retired.
Advisor: Okay. Switching to spending—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in?

Advisor: Okay. Since you’re both retired, I want to make sure I’ve got each of you labeled correctly in my notes—Elizabeth, you’re Client 2, right?
Client 2: Yes—Elizabeth is Client 2.
Advisor: Perfect. And your role in the household—would you describe yourself as David’s spouse or partner?
Client 2: Spouse/partner.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm your work status these days—are you fully retired, or doing any part-time consulting or anything like that?
